sentences.text: Jack Arnel.
J-A-C-K A-R-N-E-L.
At birth, was Jasha Aronowicz.
Spelled J-A-S-H-A A-R-O-N-O-C-H-- N-O-W-I-C-H.
Well, my father and my mother used to refer to me as [NON-ENGLISH]--
--in Yiddish.
Well, in the United States, it was changed to Jack Aronowitz.
J-A-C-K A-R-O-N-O-W-I-T-Z.
My birthday is May the 23rd, 1929.
I was born in Vilna, Poland.
Vilna, V-I-L-N-A.
At that time, it belonged to Poland.
Right.
Yes.
I grew up in Poland-- in Vilna, Poland.
I lived with my parents and my sister.
Well, in the Vilna, [SIGHING] it was, from what I can remember, a wonderful life.
We had a-- my father had a business.
He was in the fur business.
He had a shop.
My mother had her own business.
She was a dressmaker.
And my sister and I were in school, involved in school.
And they provided a very nice life for us.

We enjoyed the many freedoms and cultural events and sports, as well as every summer we went-- my parents used to take us to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a summer place, to enjoy the summers, swimming, learning different things about the world.
It was a very, very congenial, loving family.
My mother was Chaja Aronowicz.
Chaja Gold.
My father's name was Hirsh, used to refer to him as [?
Hirshel, ?] Aronowicz.
My father's name was H-I-R-S-H A-R-O-N-O-W-I-C-Z.
And my mother's name was Chaja, C-H-A-J-A, and A-R-N-O-W-O-I-C-H-- C-Z, rather.
And her maiden name was Gold, G-O-L-D.
Sonja.
S-O-N-J-A.
That's the way they would spell.
Sonja is three years older than I.
I can't think of it off-hand.
No.
In Vilna, we lived on the street called Suboch, number 7.
It was located pretty centrally, central Vilna, in the central of the city.
S-U-B-O-C-H.
[INAUDIBLE] O-C-H, yeah.
That would be the correct spelling, I believe.
Well, it was a typical European building with a court inside.
And the court was in a square, like a square.
And as you walked through the gate, we lived right opposite the gate, on the second floor.
I had to walk up the stairs.
And we had a, like, a four-room apartment.
And my sister and I shared a bedroom when we were little.
And we had-- the windows in the back of the apartment were facing into a garden, a beautiful garden.
And also, a Russian church was-- it belonged to the Russian church right there.
We had all kinds of beautiful flowers growing in that garden, and trees with fruits, apples and pears.
And the-- the Russian Orthodox fathers, they took care of the garden.
They also had a-- a-- a-- a house where the patriarch, the Russian patriarch lived in there.
And he was an old man.
He used to take strolls along the walks in the garden.
And over the years, the Russian monks that lived there, they befriended me and my family.
And they would-- summertime, when the windows were open, they would play games with me, and throw apples and pears through the windows to me, and call me, Jasha, Jasha, come on down.
We'll put up a ladder.
You walk down and you'll walk with us.
We'll take you to the church and show you how we make candles and soap and things of that nature.
That was the view from my bedroom, in the-- in the backyard.
Well, out the front, if you looked out the front, the kitchen had a big window facing the gate.
And we had-- actually it was a square.
And on either side of the square, there were stairs leading to apartments.
It was two story high.
And we had neighbors all around us.
A variety of people, mostly Polish.
And-- and quite a few Jewish.
It was all mixed.
And we had some very nice neighbors.
I befriended a lot of the Polish youth over there.
They were good friends of mine.
Yes.
There were a few special children that I played with every single day.
Well, both.
Jewish and Polish.
And at the time, when we were little, they were very interested, especially the Polish children, were very interested in our holidays.
And they asked me that I give them Matzo for-- at the-- at the time of Passover.
And when I was a youngster, I did not really feel any of the anti-Semitism, which of course, it was very-- it was becoming very prevalent as the years went on.
Yes.
I went to school.
My [SIGHS] mother and my father enrolled me into a cheder.
They wanted me to make sure that I learned the Hebrew as well as the history of our Jewish people.
And it was a private school.
I had to pay for it.
And it was rather modern, very up to date for that period, the time.
Vilna in itself was a very progressive town.
And the Jewish population was quite advanced as far as the Jewish seminaries for teachers, cantorial schools.
They produced a lot of teachers and-- and cantors, which then traveled all over the world.
The cultural events were tremendous.
We had the sports, the Maccabees clubs.
And we played soccer.
And generally speaking, the entire life in Vilna, the Jewish-- for the-- for the Jews was very, very-- a happy one.
Well, my school was the Perlman's Cheder.
Mr.
Perlman was the owner of it.
And I would say, quite a lot of the Jewish children that attended there were from a sort of a higher echelon, financially speaking, attended that school.
No.
This was merely a school for boys only.
And my sister went to a public school.
And then she went to the gymnasium, which was the-- the high school of the-- at the time there.
We had a lot of Jewish schools there.
And a lot of-- a lot of the subjects were taught in Yiddish.
There was the gymnasium, the [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium, [NON-ENGLISH] gymnasium, that all the sciences and all the math and engineering was all taught in Yiddish.
And it was-- it produced a highly intellectual and a highly skillful students graduated from there.
Well, I went only till the fifth grade, I believe.
Because that's when all hell broke out and it was interrupted.
Yes.
I belonged to a-- the-- the Zionist organizations in Vilna were very prevalent, of all kinds.
I belonged to the Betar.
And we were being indoctrinated to think about a-- an independent country in Palestine, Israel.
We learned a lot about our history and a lot about our leaders that were traveling to Israel.
At that time, it was actually Palestine.
And that was a very satisfying feeling.
Because even though we were very comfortable in Vilna, and we were Jews, but somehow, the feeling of missing our own country was always there.
Oh, no.
That was a separate clubs, they had rented just a-- a facility.
And we used to have meetings.
We used to learn to sing songs in Hebrew and in Yiddish.
And we learned to play instruments.
They provided us with-- with sports activities.
And we used to play soccer against other clubs of a similar nature.
And we were free to do as we pleased-- at that level, that is.
I know that when it got to the higher education, in the-- in the universities, the-- things were different.
The Poles were making all kinds of restrictions and the anti-Semitism has-- has shown itself much more.
Well, my father had a shop, a fur shop.
He was a master furrier himself.
And he used to do fur coats for private people as well as contracting for stores that dealt in the fur coats.
And he employed quite a few people, as well as he also made sure to take care of his own nephews.
And he taught them the trade.
And he was sent to-- his shop was right on-- in the middle of the center of the town.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH], which is actually-- meaning the wide street.
That's the way they refer it to.
He was a very dedicated man to his business and to his family.
And he, even though at that time, it was very costly to teach somebody the trade, he made sure to take care of his nephews, [INAUDIBLE].
Well, it was his father's son, and also my mother had three-- three sisters.
Some of their sons were also involved in it.
He trained them to become furriers.
Well, I was a little too young at the time.
Although I used to come up there quite often, especially many times after school.
I used to stop by and everybody there used to pick me up and put me on the high stools that they had and tell me jokes and stories, and made me laugh.
And they used to have some foxes there.
They used to make believe they're alive.
And it was a very jovial and a very happy time.
My father was very loved by his employee-- employees.
And well, I recall the time, at one time, when there was a strike, all the furriers were striking against the owners.
My father's employees, they felt so dedicated to him, that instead of striking, they slept in the shop.
They didn't go out.
They locked everything up and they continued with their work.
They just could not see hurting him that much.
[SIGHS] My mother had a-- was a-- also a wonderful craftsperson.
She was a very talented dressmaker.
Suits and coats as well.
And she had a very-- she had a shop.
One of the rooms in our apartment was the-- her shop at that time.
But she only dealt with a-- with only a clientele, and the high echelons of the city, the-- the-- the elite of the city were part of her customers at the time.
She was very selective who she did the work for.
And she had people working for him-- for her.
And she had quite a successful business of her own at the time.
She was very much sought after.
Yes.
In-- due to the fact that she ran her own business and my father was quite busy, we had a live-in maid, by the name of Josefina.
And we had, actually, at different times, we had a few others.
But Josefina was the main one.
She was there the longest.
As a matter of fact, almost up to the beginning of the World War II.
And she also employed a governess that took care of my sister and myself.
And they were both Polish.
And they were very dedicated, very loving.
And we loved them very dearly.
It's a matter of fact, to the point where my mother completely relied on them in some of the-- part of the upbringing.
And since they were also devout Catholics, on Sunday, they used to go to church.
The governess used to take me along.
And I recall, I was a little boy, and used to go to church on Sunday with her many times, and had to kneel, of course.
And the father passed by and even put the holy water over my head.
And it was-- I did it with a lot of respect for her and for her religion.
And on Yontiff and Shabbat, my-- I went with my father to the synagogue.
No.
The--
--priests know that you were not--
No.
There were many, many people there.
And nobody really knew or asked any questions like that.
My governess just explained the procedures.
Of course, I was not familiar with it.

And not only did she take me to church, but of course, we used to go to the ice skating rink, she used to take us, and used to take us for walks to the-- to the parks of the city of Vilna had several beautiful parks.
And--
Her name was Helcia.
And that's the one that I remember the most.
She was the one with us the longest.
And it was total dedication to the family.
And when one of them got married, my mother made her the gown.
And my mother supplied most of the things that she needed for marriage.
Well, in my home, with my father, my mother always spoke Yiddish at home.
But with my governess and some of my friends, was Polish.
Those were the two language that I was fluent [INAUDIBLE] at that time.
Well, they knew quite a bit, you know.
But not-- we didn't communicate with them in Yiddish.
We usually spoke in Polish.
But they understood quite a bit.
And they knew a lot of the language, especially when it came to holidays and cooking certain-- to prepare certain meals and all that.
They knew all about the Yiddish traditions.
Well, my father, he was a-- he was not very Orthodox, although we had a kosher home.
And we had a-- we attended the synagogue many times on Saturdays.
And also on all the holidays, we always went to the synagogue.
He belonged to a synagogue which was actually supported by all the furriers of the town.
And so not only was there a jovial time of celebrating the holiday, but it is also a-- a gathering place.
And that they would see their friends from the fur industry and have discussions.
And it was a very nice way of seeing each other at holidays.
Yes.
My mother used to light the candles at Friday, Friday nights.
And even the housemaid also helped with it.
She used to set it all up for us.
And we were all together, including the housemaid.
And many times the governess, she would join us and celebrate Friday nights and the special Friday night dinners.
Many times.
Many times.
My mother especially enjoyed to entertain.
And they had friends, especially, also the relatives.
My mother had three sisters and my father had a brother and a sister.
And they all-- most of them had their own families.
And the intermingling and used to go to each other houses and spend many times together.
I loved my cousins.
We used to play games together.
And it was one big, happy family.
Most of the cooking, I must say it was the housemaid, under the supervision and instructions of my mother.
And-- because my mother ran the business and she couldn't always do that.
Well, my sister, yes.
She would do that voluntarily, here and there.
And as a matter of fact, she took great pride when I was a little boy to get up on a stool and cook some breakfast for me.
And, yes.
I remember eggs.
Made fried eggs for me.
And of course, the-- my mother or the housemaid, she used to stand from afar and check it out and make sure that she doesn't get hurt or do any kind of harm.
Oh, it was so wonderful-- it was my only sister.
Of course, like all siblings, there were times where we had a run-in with each other.
Sometimes-- I recall the time when my father brought a bicycle, the first bicycle for my sister.
He brought it at summertime in the resort area there in-- in Niemenczyn.
It was called Niemenczyn.
Outside of the city.
Well, it's a difficult one.
But I'll try.
N-I-E-M-E-N-C-H-Y-N.
Niemenczyn.
It is still an-- I understand, a resort area, big resort area.
And so he brought a bicycle there.
And she wouldn't let me ride the bicycle.
I wanted-- I was dying to get on the bicycle, learn how to ride it.
And so things like that, among siblings.
We used to argue and fight.
She finally put a lock on it.
And my mother pleaded with her to allow me to ride.
And she wouldn't.
So my mother put a lock on, too.
So the bicycle stood there for [LAUGHS] a week or so with two locks on.
So that's just a little example of how life was at-- at a time when we were so young.
Every summer, during the summer, when the school was ended.
My father used to rent [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was not just us.
But some of our family, relatives and friends used to rent-- the entire area was filled with these [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] rental places.
And so people used to come and then it was near a-- a river where we used to go swimming.
And they had the beaches right alongside the river.
Oh, it was for a few months-- for the entire length of the summer.
My father-- no.
My father had-- used to come only on the weekends over there.
And he used to travel on a bus.
Because cars at that time were not really available to the individuals, even the-- only extremely wealthy people were able to possess cars.
It was-- the years were 19-- from what I remember-- 1933 to '39, that period that I remember the most.
I heard of it.
But personally, I must say, myself, I was never affected by it.
Because I had wonderful friends.
My parents always told me that I can invite my friends to play with me in my house.
And they didn't discriminate whether they were Polish or Jewish, you know.
And so I didn't-- personally, I didn't feel.
But I knew of the growing anti-Semitism in-- in Vilna.
Because I had some older cousins who went to the University of Vilna.
And I remember my aunts telling the story when they were visiting with us how they discriminated against the Jewish students.
And they only-- they put on a quota.
They would only allow 5% of the-- of the student body to be Jewish.
And then they-- they instituted a-- a way where the Jewish students had to sit only on the left side of the-- of the rooms, to segregate and separate them from the others.
And they also, I would imagine-- there was a lot of jealousy because there were a lot of Jewish businesspeople who were successful.
And-- so anti-Semitism-- they also restricted Jews from holding certain government jobs.
They did not want to-- the Jews to be in-- in control of certain agencies.
They really tried very much to [?
Polinize ?] Vilna, so that it would be completely controlled and ran by Poles.
